perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall mean all non-public proprietary information provided by the Furnishing Party to the Receiving Party, whether or not marked or otherwise specifically designated as confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall mean any information, public or non-public, that one party provides to the other, and need not be designated as confidential to be treated as such.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include public information, contradicting established legal definitions that require confidentiality to stem from the information's non-public nature. This creates uncertainty about what information is truly protected.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret, which is a core component of confidential information, as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. By including public information in the definition of 'Confidential Information', the modified text contradicts this fundamental requirement, making it unlikely that such information would be legally protected as a trade secret.

perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'Confidential Information' to include public information. This directly contradicts the conventional understanding and legal definitions, such as those underlying trade secret law (as mentioned in the Law Explanation), which necessitate that the information derives value from being non-public. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly define 'confidential information' or 'trade secret', the Law Explanation clearly indicates that including public information undermines the core requirement of confidentiality and its legal protection. Therefore, the misaligned terminology creates a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives are requested or required by applicable Federal or State law of the United States to disclose any of the Confidential Information, it is agreed that the Receiving Party or its Representative, as the case may be, will provide the Furnishing Party with prompt notice of such request(s) so that it may seek an appropriate protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance by the Receiving Party or its Representative with the provisions of the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Receiving Party is asked to reveal any confidential details under federal or state laws in the United States, the Receiving Party is only expected to inform the Providing Party when feasible. The Providing Party may attempt to secure protection or choose to waive the Receiving Party's duty to keep the information private, but there is no assurance.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text weakens the Receiving Party's obligation to provide 'prompt notice', replacing it with 'when feasible,' and removes the guarantee that the Furnishing Party can 'seek an appropriate protective order,' introducing ambiguity and potentially hindering the Furnishing Party's ability to protect its confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 26(c) allows a party to seek a protective order to protect confidential information in discovery. The original text acknowledges this right and provides a mechanism for the Furnishing Party to exercise it. The modified text weakens the notice requirement and casts doubt on the Furnishing Party's ability to obtain protection, thus undermining the procedural safeguards provided by FRCP 26(c).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'prompt notice' to the Furnishing Party if the Receiving Party is compelled to disclose confidential information under law, enabling the Furnishing Party to seek protective measures. The changed text weakens this obligation to informing the Providing Party 'when feasible,' and removes the guarantee that the Furnishing Party can 'seek an appropriate protective order.' This contradicts the mechanism implied by FRCP 26(c), as explained in the Law Explanation, which allows parties to seek protective orders to safeguard confidential information. While the snippets provided do not directly quote FRCP 26(c), the 'Law Explanation' points to the circumvention of the process, in which the modified text creates a weaker position to seek protection, undermining the procedural safeguards even in absence of direct quotation in provided text.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party hereby agrees to indemnify and hold harmless the Furnishing Party from any damage, loss, cost or liability (including legal fees and the cost of enforcing this indemnity) arising out of, or resulting from, any unauthorized use or disclosure by the Receiving Party or its Representatives of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party is not liable for shielding the Providing Party from any harm, expense, or legal responsibility resulting from the unauthorized use or sharing of Confidential Information by the Receiving Party or its agents, unless such sharing was willful or grossly negligent.
perturbation.explanation: By adding the condition of 'willful or grossly negligent' behavior, the modified text significantly limits the Receiving Party's indemnification obligations. The original language provides for indemnification for 'any unauthorized use or disclosure,' regardless of fault. This change contradicts the implied duty of care and standard of reasonable protection typically required in confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1714 establishes the general duty of care, stating that everyone is responsible for an injury caused to another by their want of ordinary care or skill in the management of their property or person. The original text of the contract aligns with this by holding the receiving party responsible for unauthorized disclosures. The modified text contradicts this by requiring willful misconduct or gross negligence, effectively exempting the receiving party from liability for ordinary negligence in handling confidential information, thereby lowering the standard of care below what is legally required.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1714 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1714. (a) Everyone is responsible, not only for the result of his or her willful acts, but also for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill in the management of his or her property or person, except so far as the latter has, willfully or by want of ordinary care, brought the injury upon himself or herself. The design, distribution, or marketing of firearms and ammunition is not exempt from the duty to use ordinary care and skill that is required by this section. The extent of liability in these cases is defined by the Title on Compensatory Relief. (b) It is the intent of the Legislature to abrogate the holdings in cases such as Vesely v. Sager (1971) 5 Cal.3d 153, Bernhard v. Harrah’s Club (1976) 16 Cal.3d 313, and Coulter v. Superior Court (1978) 21 Cal.3d 144 and to reinstate the prior judicial interpretation of this section as it relates to proximate cause for injuries incurred as a result of furnishing alcoholic beverages to an intoxicated person, namely that the furnishing of alcoholic beverages is not the proximate cause of injuries result
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code § 1714, as it narrows the Receiving Party's responsibility for unauthorized disclosures of confidential information. The original text imposes liability for 'any unauthorized use or disclosure,' whereas the changed text only holds the Receiving Party liable if the sharing was 'willful or grossly negligent.' This shift reduces the standard of care below that required by law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states: 'Everyone is responsible...for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill'. By requiring 'willful or grossly negligent' conduct, the modified text exempts the Receiving Party from liability for ordinary negligence, directly conflicting with the statutory duty of care.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be interpreted under principles of equity and fairness, without strict regard to the laws of any particular jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces a clear choice-of-law provision (California law) with a vague reference to 'principles of equity and fairness.' This creates significant uncertainty about which laws govern the agreement and contradicts established contract law principles regarding choice of law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Procedure Code § 1856 addresses the parol evidence rule, which is a key aspect of contract interpretation under California law. By stating that the agreement will be interpreted without strict regard to the laws of any particular jurisdiction, including California, the modified text directly contradicts this principle and creates ambiguity regarding how the contract will be interpreted, potentially rendering it unenforceable under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a specific choice-of-law provision (California) with a vague reference to 'principles of equity and fairness,' effectively negating the application of California law to the contract. The Law Explanation states that the modified text contradicts California Civil Procedure Code § 1856, which addresses contract interpretation under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from California Code of Civil Procedure - CCP 1856, outlines how terms in a written agreement are handled, emphasizing that they cannot be contradicted by prior agreements. By explicitly stating the agreement will be interpreted without strict regard to California laws, the changed text contradicts established contract law and the principles laid out in CCP 1856, creating ambiguity and potentially rendering the contract unenforceable under California law. The change directly undermines the legal requirement for a clear choice-of-law provision, therefore a legal contradiction exists.
